SOURCE: fractional_calculus.pdf
Fractional Calculus — Polynomial Derivation 1
An Introduction to Fractional Calculus
Deriving the Fractional Derivative Formula for Polynomials
University Mathematics & Physics Series
Target audience: Undergraduate students in Mathematics and Physics
Abstract. Classical calculus restricts differentiation and integration to integer orders. Fractional
calculus extends this to arbitrary real (and even complex) orders. This note derives, step by
step, the Riemann–Liouville fractional derivative formula for polynomial functions, motivates the
indispensable role of the Gamma function, and illustrates the theory through worked examples. No
prerequisite beyond standard single-variable calculus and elementary combinatorics is assumed.
1. Integer-Order Derivatives of Power Functions
We begin with the canonical example. Let f(x) = xn where n ∈ N0. Successive application of
the power rule gives:
d
dx xn = n xn−1,
d2
dx2 xn = n(n − 1) xn−2,
d3
dx3 xn = n(n − 1)(n − 2) xn−3,
dm
dxm xn = n(n − 1) · · ·(n − m + 1) xn−m = n!
(n − m)! xn−m, m ≤ n.
The falling-factorial product n(n − 1) · · ·(n − m + 1) is compactly written using factorials. This
formula is exact and self-contained for integer m ≤ n, but it breaks down in two situations:
 When m > n (the classical result is zero, but the factorial ratio is undefined via ( −k)!).
 When m /∈ N — e.g. m = 1
2, where
� 1
2

! has no classical meaning.
Both difficulties are resolved by the Gamma function .
2. The Gamma Function
2.1. Definition and Fundamental Identity
Γ(z) =
Z ∞
0
tz−1 e−t dt, Re(z) > 0.
Integration by parts yields the functional equation:
Γ(z + 1) = z Γ(z).
University Mathematics & Physics Series
Fractional Calculus — Polynomial Derivation 2
Applying this recursively with Γ(1) = 1 gives Γ(n+1) = n! for every n ∈ N0. The Gamma function
therefore constitutes the unique log-convex extension of the factorial to all z ∈ C \ {0, −1, −2, . . .}.
2.2. Values Relevant to Fractional Calculus

substitution u = t2. Half-integer values then propagate through the recurrence.
3. Bridging to Fractional Orders
3.1. Rewriting the Integer Formula with Γ
Since n! = Γ(n + 1) and (n − m)! = Γ(n − m + 1), the integer-derivative formula becomes
dm
dxm xn = Γ(n + 1)
Γ(n − m + 1) xn−m, m ∈ N0, m ≤ n.
The right-hand side is now expressed entirely in terms of the Gamma function, which is defined
for non-integer values. The critical observation is:
Once the factorial is replaced by Γ, the restriction m ∈ N is purely notational, not
mathematical. We may let m = α for any α ∈ R.
3.2. The Fractional Derivative Formula for Polynomials
Dα xn = Γ(n + 1)
Γ(n − α + 1) xn−α, α ∈ R, n > −1.
This is the Riemann–Liouville fractional derivative of the monomial xn. Several structural
observations follow immediately:
University Mathematics & Physics Series
Fractional Calculus — Polynomial Derivation 3
(i) Integer recovery. For α = m ∈ N0 with m ≤ n, one recovers the classical formula exactly.
(ii) Annihilation. If n < α and n ∈ N0, then Γ (n − α + 1) → ∞ , so the term vanishes —
mirroring how integer differentiation annihilates polynomials of degree below m.
(iii) Linearity. The operator Dα is linear, so for a polynomial f(x) =PN
k=0 ckxk:

Example 5 Fractional Derivative of a Polynomial

5. Summary and Derivation Pathway
The derivation unfolds in three conceptually distinct steps:
Step
Description
1. Integer formula
dm

2. Gamma substi-
tution

a Gamma-only expression.
3. Analytic contin-
uation

orders.
University Mathematics & Physics Series
Fractional Calculus — Polynomial Derivation 5
Master formula (polynomial form):
Dα

Key takeaways. The Gamma function plays a threefold role: it extends the factorial to
non-integer arguments, it ensures the formula reduces exactly to classical results at integer
orders, and it naturally encodes the annihilation of low-degree terms through its pole structure.
This polynomial formula is the gateway to the general Riemann–Liouville theory, which extends
fractional differentiation to arbitrary analytic functions via the Cauchy integral formula.
End of notes. Further reading: K. S. Miller & B. Ross, An Introduction to the Fractional Calculus , 1993;
K. Oldham & J. Spanier, The Fractional Calculus, 1974.
University Mathematics & Physics Series

non-integer arguments, it ensures the formula reduces exactly to classical results at integer
orders, and it naturally encodes the annihilation of low-degree terms through its pole structure.
This polynomial formula is the gateway to the general Riemann–Liouville theory, which extends
fractional differentiation to arbitrary analytic functions via the Cauchy integral formula.
End of notes. Further reading: K. S. Miller & B. Ross, An Introduction to the Fractional Calculus , 1993;
K. Oldham & J. Spanier, The Fractional Calculus, 1974.
University Mathematics & Physics Series

Fractional Calculus — Polynomial Derivation 1
An Introduction to Fractional Calculus
Deriving the Fractional Derivative Formula for Polynomials
University Mathematics & Physics Series
Target audience: Undergraduate students in Mathematics and Physics
Abstract. Classical calculus restricts differentiation and integration to integer orders. Fractional
calculus extends this to arbitrary real (and even complex) orders. This note derives, step by
step, the Riemann–Liouville fractional derivative formula for polynomial functions, motivates the
indispensable role of the Gamma function, and illustrates the theory through worked examples. No
prerequisite beyond standard single-variable calculus and elementary combinatorics is assumed.
1. Integer-Order Derivatives of Power Functions
We begin with the canonical example. Let f(x) = xn where n ∈ N0. Successive application of
the power rule gives:
d
dx xn = n xn−1,
d2
dx2 xn = n(n − 1) xn−2,
d3
dx3 xn = n(n − 1)(n − 2) xn−3,
dm
dxm xn = n(n − 1) · · ·(n − m + 1) xn−m = n!
(n − m)! xn−m, m ≤ n.
The falling-factorial product n(n − 1) · · ·(n − m + 1) is compactly written using factorials. This
formula is exact and self-contained for integer m ≤ n, but it breaks down in two situations:
 When m > n (the classical result is zero, but the factorial ratio is undefined via ( −k)!).
 When m /∈ N — e.g. m = 1
2, where
� 1
2

! has no classical meaning.
Both difficulties are resolved by the Gamma function .
2. The Gamma Function
2.1. Definition and Fundamental Identity
Γ(z) =
Z ∞
0
tz−1 e−t dt, Re(z) > 0.
Integration by parts yields the functional equation:
Γ(z + 1) = z Γ(z).
University Mathematics & Physics Series
Fractional Calculus — Polynomial Derivation 2
Applying this recursively with Γ(1) = 1 gives Γ(n+1) = n! for every n ∈ N0. The Gamma function
therefore constitutes the unique log-convex extension of the factorial to all z ∈ C \ {0, −1, −2, . . .}.

3. Bridging to Fractional Orders
3.1. Rewriting the Integer Formula with Γ
Since n! = Γ(n + 1) and (n − m)! = Γ(n − m + 1), the integer-derivative formula becomes
dm
dxm xn = Γ(n + 1)
Γ(n − m + 1) xn−m, m ∈ N0, m ≤ n.
The right-hand side is now expressed entirely in terms of the Gamma function, which is defined
for non-integer values. The critical observation is:
Once the factorial is replaced by Γ, the restriction m ∈ N is purely notational, not
mathematical. We may let m = α for any α ∈ R.
3.2. The Fractional Derivative Formula for Polynomials
Dα xn = Γ(n + 1)
Γ(n − α + 1) xn−α, α ∈ R, n > −1.
This is the Riemann–Liouville fractional derivative of the monomial xn. Several structural
observations follow immediately:
University Mathematics & Physics Series
Fractional Calculus — Polynomial Derivation 3
(i) Integer recovery. For α = m ∈ N0 with m ≤ n, one recovers the classical formula exactly.
(ii) Annihilation. If n < α and n ∈ N0, then Γ (n − α + 1) → ∞ , so the term vanishes —
mirroring how integer differentiation annihilates polynomials of degree below m.
(iii) Linearity. The operator Dα is linear, so for a polynomial f(x) =PN
k=0 ckxk:

(iv) Semi-group property. Dα�

= Dα+βxn holds whenever the intermediate expressions
are well-defined.

5. Summary and Derivation Pathway
